source: Heller House — SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen interview — financial mechanics, Starship timeline, orbital compute, AI business
summary: The terrestrial data center cost curve is deteriorating (power, cooling, land/regulatory costs rising) while orbital compute costs will improve, creating a long-term structural cost crossover.
summary_extended: Baker (via the Heller House interview with SpaceX CFO Bret Johnsen) presents a structural cost crossover thesis: terrestrial data center economics are deteriorating as power costs rise, liquid cooling grows more complex and expensive, and land/regulatory opposition intensifies, while orbital compute costs are on an improving trajectory driven by Moore's Law silicon reductions, zero land lease costs, free radiative cooling in space, and solar panels that are roughly 5x more efficient in orbit. Johnsen argues these two cost curves are moving in opposite directions and will eventually cross, making orbital compute economically competitive with — and ultimately superior to — ground-based data centers. The investment implication is a long-term structural bull case for SpaceX's orbital compute ambitions, contingent on Starship achieving sufficient launch cadence and cost reduction to make frequent payload delivery economical. The thesis is directionally compelling on first principles but carries meaningful uncertainty around the timing of the crossover and the pace of Starship scaling, making it a long-duration, high-conviction-but-uncertain structural view rather than a near-term catalyst.
detail: Johnsen explicitly contrasts the two trajectories: orbital compute benefits from silicon cost reductions (Moore's Law), no land lease, free radiative cooling, and 5x more efficient solar. Terrestrial data centers face rising power bills, increasingly complex liquid cooling, and growing regulatory/land opposition. This cost crossover is the core long-term bull case for orbital compute.
confidence_evidence: Logical first-principles argument made by CFO; directionally compelling but crossover timing is uncertain and depends on Starship scaling.
quote_fragment: terrestrial solutions the curve is going the other direction — everything getting more expensive
